text: The surgeon places a 12mm and a 5mm trocar higher than usual along the umbilicus line, with the optical device positioned between the xiphoid process and the umbilicus.
The surgeon maneuvers a large myoma, approximately fifteen centimeters in size, within the surgical field.
The surgeon attempts to control both uterine arteries at their origin by opening the pericardium lateral to the infundibulopelvic ligament, allowing the insufflated gas to assist in the dissection.
The surgeon navigates around the large myoma, searching for an optimal dissecting plane to facilitate its removal, while managing the challenges posed by the myoma's substantial volume.
The surgeon identifies the umbilical artery and ureter as landmarks and proceeds to develop the pararectal fossa, carefully navigating around these structures.
The surgeon uses traction and counter traction to locate the umbilical artery and develop the pararectal fossa, revealing these anatomical structures within the surgical field.
The surgeon develops the area adjacent to the paradigmal fossa, continuing the dissection process in the pelvic region.
The pararectal fossa is clearly visible, with the uterine artery identified as the first artery within the surgical field.
The surgeon continues dissection medially to the umbilical artery, navigating around the anatomical structures within the pararectal fossa.
The surgeon has clearly exposed both the pararectal and paramedical fossae, with visible anatomical landmarks including the umbilical artery and ureter.
The uterine artery is clearly visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon safely places a clip on the left ureter after its medialization, ensuring it is securely positioned away from the surgical field.
The surgeon applies a standard surgical clip to secure a structure, with the uterine artery and pararectal fossa visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon prepares to perform the same procedure on the opposite side, focusing on the anatomical structures similar to those previously addressed, including the uterine artery and surrounding fossae.
The surgeon tracks medially along the right infundibulopelvic ligament, ensuring clear visualization of the surrounding anatomical structures.
The surgeon opens the peritoneum laterally to the right infundibulopelvic ligament, while assistants work to increase and open the retroperitoneal space, revealing underlying structures.
The surgeon uses traction and counter-traction to push the myoma to the left, facilitating the development of the pararectal fossa.
The surgeon positions the device beneath the ureter, which is visible running above the surgical instrument.
The internal iliac artery and umbilical artery are clearly visible, along with two uterine arteries that are being prepared for control with a single clip.
The surgeon places a clip on the uterine arteries to decrease vascularization, visible within the retroperitoneal space opened earlier.
The surgeon develops both the pararectal and paramedical fossa, preparing to place a clip solely on the uterine artery, ensuring precise control of the vessel.
The surgeon places a clip perpendicularly across both uterine arteries, effectively controlling them with a single clip.
The uterus appears well devascularized, and the surgeon proceeds to make a longitudinal incision along the medial aspect using a bipolar instrument.
The surgeon progressively opens the uterine serosa and coagulates the myometrial capsule, aiming to expose the entire myoma.
The surgical field appears bloodless as the procedure progresses, indicating effective hemostatic control during the myomectomy.
The surgeon exposes a large myoma within the uterus and proceeds to enlarge the hystereotomy to accommodate the myoma's volume.
The surgeon prepares to make a ten-centimeter incision to accommodate the removal of a fifteen-centimeter myoma, ensuring the incision size is appropriate for the myoma's volume.
The surgeon prepares to use laparoscopic ten millimeter forceps to manipulate the uterine serosa and myometrial capsule, continuing the dissection towards the exposed myoma.
The surgeon positions an instrument laterally to the large myoma, preparing for further manipulation or removal.
The surgeon uses forceps to grasp and apply traction to the large myoma, attempting to extract it from its bed within the uterine tissue.
The assistants stabilize the healthy myoma trio while the surgeon attempts to extract the large myoma from its bed, adjusting the hystereotomy incision to accommodate the myoma's volume.
The surgeon applies bipolar energy to the myoma, avoiding the surrounding healthy myoma trio, using laparoscopic instruments.
The surgeon identifies a good dissecting plane around the myoma, allowing for a straightforward and bloodless myomectomy, as visible in the video.
The surgeon identifies a large vein and uses coagulation to control it, while continuing to apply traction to extract the myoma from its bed.
The assistants apply traction to the myometrium on the opposite side while the surgeon manipulates the tissue laterally, facilitating the extraction of the myoma.
The surgeon avoids using energy devices during the final stages of dissection to minimize the risk of burning the endometrial tissue.
The surgeon applies traction to the myoma, visibly completing the myomectomy procedure with minimal bleeding.
The surgeon continues the myomectomy by maintaining dissection within the correct plane, avoiding the use of energy devices inside the myoma bed to prevent tissue damage.
The surgeon has successfully removed the entire myoma from the uterus, completing the myomectomy procedure.
The surgeon cleans the myoma bed, removing blood from the rectovaginal space, and proceeds to suture the area, utilizing sutures as hemostatic tools.
The surgeon uses a 14mm needle with Vicryl suture material to perform suturing in the surgical field.
The surgeon places stitches laterally and away from the edge of the scar, effectively invaginating the healthy myometrium into the surgical site.
The surgeon achieves perfect coaptation within the myoma bed, reducing the risk of intramyometrial hematoma and post-operative bleeding, while ensuring a strong scar formation.
The assistants invaginate the healthy myometrium while the surgeon uses intracorporeal knotting technique to secure the stitches, ensuring precise control over the sutures.
The surgeon observes the ischemic edge of the scar and proceeds to place three or four stitches to secure the area.

The surgeon uses a 14mm needle with Vicryl suture to perform intracorporeal knotting, placing stitches four centimeters lateral on both the left and right sides. The technique aims to invaginate the healthy myometrium into the myoma bed.
The surgeon places five or six stitches around the fifteen-centimeter diameter myoma, ensuring proper closure and hemostasis of the surgical site.
The surgeon demonstrates good invagination of the tissue with a double flat knot technique, performing quick suturing to effectively decrease bleeding during the myomectomy procedure.
The surgeon ties the final knot on the sutures placed around the excised myoma site, ensuring secure closure and hemostasis.
The surgeon places a stitch on the posterior part of the uterine fundus, securing the tissue in the area where the myoma was removed.
The surgeon carefully places a 14mm needle into the appropriate plane of the uterine tissue, ensuring precise alignment for effective suturing.
The surgeon carefully places stitches on the uterine fundus, ensuring the rectum is not inadvertently included in the suturing process.
The surgeon demonstrates intracorporeal knotting techniques, including double flat knots, to achieve effective hemostasis and ensure a strong scar formation on the uterine fundus.
The surgeon prepares to insert a large endoscopic bag with a capacity of 1200 cc to accommodate the entire myoma within the surgical field.
The surgeon places the myoma inside a large endoscopic bag and begins morcellation within the bag using a morcellator set to seven hundred rods, reducing the risk of leiomyomatosis.
The surgeon performs morcellation of the myoma within the endoscopic bag, ensuring that no bowel tissue is inadvertently included in the morcellator under continuous visual control.
The surgeon completes the morcellation process, removes the endobag containing the morcellated tissue, and cleans the pelvic cavity.
The surgeon closes the incision site used for the morcellator to reduce the risk of incisional hernia.
